topic: Qwen 3.6 Plus Agentic Coding Guide: 1M Context Window for Complex Tasks

key_points: Qwen 3.6 Plus leads Terminal-Bench 2.0 with 61.6, surpassing Claude 4.5 Opus
Available free on OpenRouter during preview; model ID qwen/qwen3.6-plus-preview:free
Always-on CoT reasoning — no thinking/non-thinking toggle unlike Qwen3 family
Sparse MoE architecture with linear attention for efficient long-context processing
Multimodal: processes both text and images natively
65K output tokens — one of highest output limits available
1M context window — equivalent to ~2,000 pages of text or most mid-sized codebases
Flat pricing model: same rate from token 1 to token 1,000,000
Strong fit for text-heavy workloads: large codebase analysis, long-document reasoning
Performs dramatically better in agentic harness than plain chat mode
Native function calling activates multi-step reasoning capabilities
OpenCode terminal agent optimized for Qwen 3.6 Plus workflows
OpenRouter uses OpenAI-compatible API format — drop-in replacement
Function calling, JSON mode, and streaming work with OpenAI SDK clients
Recommended for repository-level analysis and security auditing

stats.fact: Qwen 3.6 Plus scores 78.8% on SWE-bench Verified, just 2 points behind Claude Opus 4.6's 80.8%
Qwen 3.6 Plus leads Terminal-Bench 2.0 with a score of 61.6, surpassing Claude 4.5 Opus
Pricing: $0.325/M input tokens and $1.95/M output tokens vs Claude Opus 4.7 at $5.00/$25.00 — output ~13x cheaper
Qwen 3.6 Plus is approximately 1.7x faster throughput than Claude and 2x faster than GPT-5.4
1M token context equals roughly 750,000 words — enough to hold most mid-sized codebases in a single prompt
OmniDocBench score of 91.2 and MMMU score of 86.0 demonstrate strong multimodal and document understanding
Flat 1M context pricing can cut bills 40-60% for long-document workloads vs tiered pricing models
Qwen-Agent framework version 0.0.34 released February 2026; supports MCP, Code Interpreter, RAG, tool calling

angles: How the 1M context window enables true repository-scale coding — loading entire codebases without chunking or summarization
